cases.name: United States v. Ramsey
cases.holding: Routine searches at the international border (including incoming international mail) require neither a warrant nor probable cause; the border-search exception rests on the sovereign's inherent authority to protect itself.

cases.name: United States v. Montoya de Hernandez
cases.cite: United States v. Montoya de Hernandez, 473 U.S. 531 (1985)
cases.holding: The prolonged detention of a suspected alimentary-canal (balloon) smuggler at the border is reasonable when customs officers have *reasonable suspicion* that the traveler is smuggling contraband internally.

quotes.text: We hold that the detention of a traveler at the border, beyond the scope of a routine customs search and inspection, is justified at its inception if customs agents, considering all the facts surrounding the traveler and her trip, reasonably suspect that the traveler is smuggling contraband in her alimentary canal.

cases.name: United States v. Flores-Montano
cases.holding: The government's authority to conduct suspicionless searches of vehicles at the border includes disassembling and reassembling a gas tank; this property search is routine and needs no reasonable suspicion.

cases.name: United States v. Martinez-Fuerte
cases.holding: Brief stops at fixed/permanent interior immigration checkpoints are constitutional without any individualized suspicion; routine questioning and referral to secondary inspection are reasonable.

cases.name: Almeida-Sanchez v. United States
cases.holding: A warrantless search of a vehicle by a roving Border Patrol on a California road at least 20 miles north of the Mexican border, without probable cause or consent, violated the Fourth Amendment. This is a roving-Border-Patrol / vehicle-search reasonableness holding — it is NOT a good-faith-exception case.

quotes.text: But the search of the petitioner's automobile by a roving patrol, on a California road that lies at all points at least 20 miles north of the Mexican border, was of a wholly different sort. In the absence of probable cause or consent, that search violated the petitioner's Fourth Amendment right to be free of "unreasonable searches and seizures."

cases.name: United States v. Touset
cases.cite: United States v. Touset, 890 F.3d 1227 (11th Cir. 2018)
cases.holding: The Fourth Amendment requires no suspicion — not even reasonable suspicion — for a forensic search of an electronic device at the border; devices are property, and reasonable suspicion is required only for highly intrusive searches of a *person's body*.
cases.treatment_note: Current 11th Cir. law; holds NO suspicion required even for forensic device searches at the border — splits with 9th Cir. (Cotterman/Cano). Persuasive only.

cases.name: United States v. Cano
cases.cite: United States v. Cano, 934 F.3d 1002 (9th Cir. 2019)
cases.holding: Manual border searches of a cell phone need no suspicion, but a *forensic* (Cellebrite-type) device search requires reasonable suspicion that the device itself contains contraband — and the border-search exception covers only searches for contraband, not evidence of past/future crimes.
cases.treatment_note: Current 9th Cir. law; limits device border searches (manual = no suspicion; forensic = reasonable suspicion) to searches for digital contraband. Persuasive only.

cases.name: United States v. Brignoni-Ponce
cases.holding: A roving Border Patrol may stop a vehicle near the border to question occupants only on reasonable suspicion, based on specific articulable facts, that they are aliens illegally in the country; apparent Mexican ancestry ALONE is not enough.

cases.name: United States v. Cotterman
cases.cite: United States v. Cotterman, 709 F.3d 952 (9th Cir. 2013) (en banc)
cases.holding: A forensic (comprehensive) examination of an electronic device seized at the border requires reasonable suspicion; it is the comprehensive/intrusive nature of the forensic search, not its location, that triggers the requirement.

quotes.text: We therefore hold that the forensic examination of Cotterman's computer required a showing of reasonable suspicion, a modest requirement in light of the Fourth Amendment.

cases.treatment_note: Still good law in the 9th Cir.; in tension with United States v. Touset, 890 F.3d 1227 (11th Cir. 2018), which requires no suspicion even for forensic device border searches. 9th Cir. later limited Cotterman's reach to data physically on the device in United States v. Cano (2019).

note: Phase-3 FINAL authoring manifest: packet(manifest+deck) + P3 gap verifications + marquee quotes + good-law.
